definition: The Grail is the sacred vessel of Arthurian legend — the object of the great quest, identified with the cup of the Last Supper. It is defined less by what it is than by its being sought: unlike an ordinary *chalice*, the Grail is the lost and holy vessel whose pursuit is the whole point, glimpsed but rarely grasped.
history: From Old French *graal*, 'cup, dish,' from Medieval Latin *gradalis*. Its sanctity was retro-fitted. In Chrétien de Troyes' *Perceval* the *graal* is a serving-dish carried in procession, not yet holy; only in the reworkings by clerical authors — above all the early-13th-century *Quest of the Holy Grail* — does it become the wonder-working chalice of the Last Supper, thick with theological meaning. The dinner-dish rose into the Holy Grail, and 'grail' now names any sought-after ultimate — the philosophers' stone as 'the Holy Grail of alchemy.'

def_sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
def_sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

commentary: The Grail's sanctity was retrofitted, and modern scholarship has learned to distrust its own myth-making. In Chrétien's *Perceval* the *graal* is an unexplained serving-dish; only when clerical authors reworked the romance, Kieckhefer observes, did the religious matter thicken into the Last Supper's chalice. The twentieth century then re-mythologized it: Jessie Weston's *From Ritual to Romance* traced the Grail to a pagan fertility cult — an argument that shaped Eliot and Frazer's afterlife but that Arthurian scholars have largely rejected as speculative. The field now stresses the Grail as a literary-Christian construction, not the survival of an archaic rite.

commentary_sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
commentary_sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

commentary_sources.label: Jessie Weston, From Ritual to Romance
commentary_sources.abstract: This early-1900s study argues that the medieval legends about the search for the Holy Grail (the sacred cup in King Arthur stories) did not start as purely Christian tales, but grew out of much older fertility rituals — ceremonies meant to keep crops, animals, and land healthy and productive. The book later became famous for directly inspiring T. S. Eliot's poem 'The Waste Land.'

etymology.origin: Old French graal ‘cup, dish’ ← Medieval Latin gradalis
etymology.note: Chrétien de Troyes’ ‘graal’ was just a serving-dish; only later did it become the Holy Grail. The word’s own sanctity was retro-fitted.
etymology.hook: The Holy Grail started as a dinner plate.

flavor_snippets.html:
"Look deep into the <mark>Grail</mark>, and see there what you desire most."
—Gerda Äagesdotter, Archmage of the Unseen
